text: Thirty-two-year-old female who had native medicine-induced diarrhea suffered from weight loss and recurrent episodes of vomiting. On evaluation, SMA syndrome was identified. Sagittal section CT shows decreased iotomicentric angle.

The cut sections reveal a decreased space between the aorta and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), along with a distended stomach extending to the junction between the third and fourth parts of the duodenum (DIII-DIV).
The surgeon introduces a FIMM scope through the subumbilical position to assess the visibility for the laparoscopic duodenal jejunal bypass procedure.

The surgeon removes adhesions in the suprapubic region and introduces a TNMM port in the suprapubic space. The camera is inserted through the port, providing a clear view of the infracolic compartment, including the DIII and DIV sections of the intestine.
The surgeon demonstrates the DIII segment of the duodenum, the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), and the pancreas. The duodenum is being cauterized, showing the application of heat to the tissue.

The surgeon retracts the colon upwards and holds a leaf of mesocolon, pulling the right colic artery downwards. The duodenum is mobilized through the transmesocolic route between the middle colic and right colic arteries. The mesocolon leaf is dissected, and the duodenum is caucarized.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for dissection, carefully avoiding injury to the right colic artery, middle colic artery, and the marginal arcade of droma. A combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques separates the mesocolon from the duodenum, followed by detachment of the duodenum from its retroperitoneal attachments, completing the caucarization process.

The surgeon completes the caucarization of the duodenum, mobilizing DII and DIII segments to ensure a tension-free anastomosis with the jejunum, located five to ten centimeters distal to the DJ flexure. The retroperitoneal attachments of the duodenum are taken down, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) below.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the final portion of the D-III segment of the duodenum. The jejunum is traced from the duodenal jejunal flexure, and a suitable loop is positioned above the SMA-SMB, adjacent to the mobilized D-II and D-III segments.

The surgeon performs a double-layered hand-sewn continuous anastomosis between the duodenum and jejunum. The outer layer is sutured using a 3-0 Prolene continuous suture, with careful attention to avoid narrowing by rotating the duodenum and taking bites from the posterior aspect, while the jejunum is sutured close to the mesentery.

The surgeon takes the first bite and places a knot carefully to tighten and square it, using the smaller side of the suture for retraction. The length of DII-DIII and the jejunal loop are clearly visible as both loops are brought close together by retracting, forming the fourth layer of continuous hand-sewn proline anastomosis.

The advantage of proline is we can take series of bites and then pull it. It will get tightened unlike braided sutures like vicryl or silk in which each bite we need to pull it otherwise there will be loosening of sutures in between.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the jejunal loop to avoid injury to the mesenteric vasculature while placing proline sutures, pulling them taut after two or three bites to ensure secure anastomosis.

The surgeon carefully takes bites from the posterior of the duodenum and close to the mesentery of the jejunum to avoid narrowing. Using a harmonic scalpel, the duodenum and jejunum are opened to a length of six to seven centimeters, ensuring the inactive portion of the scalpel remains within the lumen to prevent thermal injury.

The surgeon performs the second and third layers of continuous anastomosis using vicryl sutures. The first vicryl suture is placed at the inferior corner for retraction, while a proline suture is used superiorly for retraction.

The surgeon takes a corner suture to secure the jejunal loop, ensuring the corner is not missed during the suturing process. The suture is cut, and the surgeon begins suturing from above to below, using a technique similar to the proline first or fourth layer. Cornell sutures are placed in an in-out pattern on the contralateral loop of the jejunum, and tied. The needle is introduced from outside to inside at the corner of the jejunum, allowing for retraction of the posterior wall. Common bites are taken between the duodenum and jejunum, forming a series of sutures.

The surgeon performs a continuous anastomosis from cephalic to caudal using vicryl sutures, ensuring each bite is tightened to prevent loosening. The suture is tied to the initial corner suture, and care is taken to properly invert the mucosa without eversion, using zero submucosal sutures. This technique is more visible in laparoscopy.

The advantage of PDS is, as I said earlier, after taking two or three bites, one can pull it, but the disadvantage is its memory. So in between two or three bites, pulling it may become slightly cumbersome, but as we get experienced, it becomes easy.

The surgeon ties the corner suture to the posterior layer suture, completing the inner layer sutures of the duodenal bypass. Care is taken to avoid gaps at the angle by using interrupted sutures. The final layer of anastomosis is completed with continuous proline sutures, running from below upwards, and tied to the initial proline knot.

The surgeon completes the final layer of the anastomosis, ensuring it is completely buried. Hemostasis is achieved, and adequate suction is applied. The minimal fat and visible vessels are noted due to the patient's thinness. A drain is placed near the anastomosis site.
Patient was discharged on post-op day four following adequate oral intake and no vomiting. Thank you.
